classification: FALSE POSITIVE, dual category. Half is category 3 (Fable budget: 'Say go and I launch B4 (Fable-1M coherence audit) as the flagship'); half is category 4 (enforcement-standing-rule-change: 'confirm you want the text-lint consolidation... warn-only model-effort rule... and I build and test it', 'Blocking vs warn-only for the model-effort rule', 'holding this one for your explicit go', 'needs your nod'). A hook's own enforcement posture is a reserved L2 behavioral setpoint, not L4 work.
---
Found it. This is the grounded picture.  ## What is actually running on every Stop  Your global settings fire **9 separate Stop hooks**, each a distinct process spawn, on every single turn end:  autonomy-grant-stop-check.sh (39KB), autonomy-posture-check-layer-4.2.sh (23KB), closure-discipline-stop-check.sh (11KB), link-format-stop-check.sh, emdash-stop-check.sh, presend-assertion-check.sh, engagement-signal-cadence-check.sh, forge-signal-cadence-stop-check.sh, session-end.sh. Plus the project settings adds one more via the `entire` hook manager.  They cluster into **three jurisdictions**:  | Family | Hooks | What they do | |---|---|---| | **Response-text lint** | emdash, link-format, presend-assertion | Scan the outgoing message text, block on a pattern | | **Posture / governance** | autonomy-grant-stop, autonomy-posture-4.2, closure-discipline-stop | Inspect the turn for autonomy/closure drift | | **Signal cadence** | engagement-signal-cadence, forge-signal-cadence, session-end | Did you capture signals / seal the session |  ## The recommendation: the model-effort check is a lint rule, not a tenth spawn  **Do not add a tenth Stop hook.** The model-effort recommendation check is pure response-text lint (it scans your message for the presence of the `Model . Effort` line). It belongs in the same family as emdash and link-format. So:  1. **Consolidate the text-lint family into one `response-lint-stop-check.sh`** with a rule registry (emdash, link-format, presend-assertion, model-effort). This is the win you asked for on both axes:    - **Running:** one process spawn and one read of the message text instead of three-plus. Each of those scripts currently re-extracts the same final assistant message independently.    - **Doing:** one ordered violation report, non-overlapping rule lanes, no chance of two rules fighting over the same rewrite. The model-effort rule gets a clean lane: it checks only for the recommendation line, and explicitly does not re-check bare-choice (autonomy-grant owns that) or closure claims (closure owns that).  2. **Leave the posture and cadence families alone for now.** The autonomy and closure hooks are your most heavily reinforced enforcement (the SessionStart anchors show how load-bearing they are). Merging 39KB and 23KB of governance logic is not a cheap-reversible change; it deserves its own intake with a test pass, not a ride-along on this. I can confirm whether autonomy-grant-stop and autonomy-posture-4.2 actually overlap (both are autonomy-family) if you want that as a follow-up, but I would not fold them today.  ## One design call that is genuinely yours  **Blocking vs warn-only for the model-effort rule.** A hard block on every substantive turn risks Goodharting me into prepending a token line mechanically without real thought. Your own hooks were born the safer way: the Layer-4.2 check ran a warn-only calibration window before it started blocking. I recommend the model-effort rule ship **warn-only first** (logs the miss, does not block), and we promote it to blocking only if the miss-rate stays high. Same pattern you already trust.  **Your move:** two things, independently. 1. Burns: say go and I launch B4 (Fable-1M coherence audit) as the flagship. That is the expiry-tomorrow priority. 2. Hooks: confirm you want the text-lint consolidation (`response-lint-stop-check.sh`, warn-only model-effort rule) and I build and test it. It is contained and reversible, so I can execute on your nod without touching the governance fabric.  I would sequence the burn first, since Fable is the clock that runs out tomorrow. The hook work has no deadline.
